_label: Cycloïdebaan met verstelbare rechte helling en cycloïde slinger

identified_by.content: Cycloïdebaan met verstelbare rechte helling en cycloïde slinger

referred_to_by.content: This is an 18th-century physics demonstration instrument that embodies two of the most elegant discoveries in the history of mathematics: the **brachistochrone** (curve of fastest descent) and the **tautochrone** (curve of equal-time descent) — both of which turn out to be the same curve: the **cycloid**. The apparatus has an adjustable straight incline (to compare against a cycloidal track) and a cycloidal pendulum (demonstrating that a weight swings in equal time regardless of amplitude). It was built by **J. Kampman** in the workshop of **Adam Steitz** in Amsterdam, following the instrument designs of **Willem Jacob 's Gravesande** (1688–1742), the foremost promoter of Newtonian physics on the European continent. The instrument connects three giants of 17th-century science: **Christiaan Huygens** (who discovered the tautochrone property in 1673), **Johann Bernoulli** (who posed the brachistochrone challenge in 1696), and **Isaac Newton** (who solved it in a single evening). It is part of a coordinated suite of 's Gravesande-style mechanics apparatus at Teylers, all from the Kampman/Steitz workshop.
